I then moved in to, and as I was awaiting, they had kind of classes.

can.

Yeah, and great product.

I really thrive in spaces where I can be a little bit closer to strategy and take more ownership over my process and grow there.

You know, their own strategy and their own way of selling because every rep is very different.

That's a little bit more authentic to ourselves.
And that is something that was missing at Common Room for me.

was like the worst experience in my life.

And I was like, I'm all.

think it's important because in a lot of my past roles, like one of my favorite parts of the companies I've worked at is the people on my team.

So moving into some of these like standard questions, the first one is like, how did, have you positioned your product's value?

And I know you haven't maybe sold to too many of these leaders other than common room to let, let's use common room maybe, but how have you positioned the value of the product to a CMO or like a go-to-market leader and a deal?
How do you, how do you position that?
Yeah.
Like CMO for on first call throughout the whole process.
Yeah.
Like they're trying to understand, okay, what's the value of using common room?
What's, what's your answer to that?
Yeah, so the way that I like to sell is more thinking about myself as an educator in the space rather than looking to sell them.
So I think what's really important for anyone is, you know, you and I might be buying the same products, but we have very different reasons for buying them.

So first call, even before the first call, understanding, you know, what the company strategy might look like, what their overall goals are individually, what the company goals are, right, what their team goals may be.
Not all of those may be aligned.

So I think that's really important to understand what business initiatives are driving, you know, why they might want to purchase a solution to understand that so that I have more of a foundation of what questions to ask on the first call.
I think every single call you do has to have discovery in it because things change and things progress.
But really.
Really understanding what their problems are and then creating that value and every call aligning that value to the problem that we're solving for them.
So I think, you know, what I've learned at Common Room that is, I think, really important is a lot of, there's so many tools out there, especially in the AI space, right?

And so a lot of buyers are a little overwhelmed with all the tools that are, and they're also, I don't know if you come across this, but they're also very suspicious of AI and suspicious of tools and the, you know, the promise of AI versus the reality of AI.
And I think that it's really important to almost say, hey, this is your problem.
This is exactly how we're going to solve it.
And now here are the solutions that I think that would be really helpful in providing options so that the buyer is not overwhelmed with decision fatigue and making, you know.
figuring out what would work best for them rather than laying it out for them and telling them what they should do.
I've found that buyers respect that a lot more and it makes you more transparent and more authentic in the selling process rather than, you know, hey, you should have this because of X, Y, and Z.
And this is how we solve this because of X, Y, and Z.

Like that is more scripty and less authentic, less transparent rather than, hey, I know my stuff, this is what you should do and here's how we can solve this problem for your business and here's where we've done it multiple times to provide that.

So in speaking with anyone in C-suite, right, it's really important that you align not only how we can help their problem, but like how that ties back to the business initiative because ultimately the CFO is probably going to have, need to see it and need to approve it, right?
Even some of the smaller companies, the CEO is going to be directly involved or the founder or whoever.
So making sure.
That our problem not only ties back to the CMO and what they're looking to solve, but it's a win-win to tie back to the business initiative.
And I think that's important to do on every call.
I love that perspective.
I definitely agree with a lot of what you said.
I guess more tactically with Common Room, like what metrics or outcomes would Common Room help the CMO or the business drive?
Yeah.
And that was too, like where a lot of that research really came into play because there's so many ways that Common Room can help a business, particularly for a CMO, right?

So that, you know, if I'm a marketing team, I'm able to say, hey, SDRs and AEs, here are your, here are your leads.
This is why they're important.
Here are the pages that they're researching on.
So now you can tailor a very specific message to that individual rather than it being a, hey, congrats on the new role kind of message that you're sending out, right?

And so there's a few, you know, there's a few more caveats to how common room helps marketing teams, but that's an area where we understand, okay, what is the problem you're looking to solve?
They could.
Care less maybe about events and just focus on LinkedIn.
Maybe it's ad attribution, whatever it may be, to really bring that in.
Does that answer your question?

And Common Room helps facilitate that by surfacing all the intent data from all these disparate sources.

was like, Hey, like they were in Toronto.

like, okay, settle down.

If you're there in person, they will.

But went up there, created a great relationship, and then it took a little bit of time, but was able to work in and get multiple hospitals on board, which was a really big expansion opportunity, a great logo for BlueSight.
At BlueSight, we sold product-specific, so that kind of foot in the door allowed us to then get our whole suite of products in there at this specific health system in LA.
So that was one, really.
It was more so just forming and keeping a great relationship.
With, you know, the decision makers and people who were going to be signing the contract who could then help influence down, which was really important there.
At Common Room, we did a lot of trials and everything.
So a lot of that was focused on the last trial deal that I closed there, which sometimes the smaller deals are usually the biggest pains.
I'm sure you're, you can relate in the deal cycle, but really focused.
I don't know if you guys do any trials today, but we were trying to stay away from them, but it was the inevitable.
From discovery on, you know, focus on finding the right people, getting them in early.
I think mapping the org is really important so that on the first call, you can bring people up by name and say, hey, how do they feel about this?
Do we bring them in, right?
Like, ultimately.
Do we bring them in, ultimately.
At Common Room, we needed RevOps.
We needed marketing and sales all aligned in order for the deal to progress.
So we knew we needed to bring them in early.
A lot of those ended up in trials, right?

So having the trial, I would create a Slack channel with each of my potential customers that were in the trial, following up on them, sending them leads, sending them different collateral that was important and helpful for them, not just spamming them with case studies.
But saying, hey, this relates to you, and this is what you should be doing.
Here's a lead.
Hey, this person's on.
And really staying close the entire time to keep forming that relationship, which worked with me well.
And a few of my, like, in the last few months, a few of my trials closed because of that, because they were very weary, weren't going to sign.
And I'm like, actually, you have all these people.
Like, this is like hundreds of thousands of dollars you're leaving on the table, which kind of puts it in person.

And more of what I was speaking on earlier of sometimes you just need to show people, hey, this is what you need to do and this is your, you know, what's going to be most beneficial for you so that they're able to conceptualize it rather than have decision fatigue.

So two different use cases, one from BlueSight, common room, more focused on the trial perspective, but I think that keeping your relationships and being authentic throughout the whole deal cycle is the most important in our day of AI that we're in today.
Definitely.

So...
So...

Like we can sell you these individual products, but this is what it looks like as a whole suite.
And I think wrapping that all together was a lot more intriguing to them rather than going with some of our competitors who some of them were cheaper, right?
With a cheaper product, you get a cheaper solution and really wrapping that up for them was important.

go.
